clusters.claim: S1's coaching-resistant, SES-aware admission reform is supportable only if it removes informal selection and expands access, but must not be rolled out before the mechanism is validated.
clusters.interest: Behind this cluster is a broad coalition interest — parents, equity advocates, employers, unions, researchers, and government reformers — in a reform that actually reduces the SES-skewed test-prep arms race rather than merely relabelling the same selection machinery.
clusters.value: The tension is equity (expanding access for able low-SES/Roma pupils) versus reliability/procedural integrity (not deploying an unvalidated SES proxy or unproven coaching-resistant assessment that could reproduce or legitimise the existing skew).
clusters.fear: The shared fear is a theatrical reform that swaps one opaque selection mechanism for another — informal selection re-emerging via interviews and discretionary criteria while the reform claims to have solved the problem — leaving children under the same pressure and inequality under a new label.
affected: low-SES and Roma pupils and their families
teachers in selective and general schools
clusters.assumption: The claim rests on the assumption that a gaming-resistant, validated SES indicator and a demonstrably less-coachable assessment can be constructed, and that a pilot can reliably detect informal-selection substitution before nationwide rollout.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: no validated SES indicator currently exists in Hungarian administrative data, the causal claim that a redesigned exam changes who applies rests on disputed evidence (Kertesi & Kezdi vs. Horn), and coaching-resistance has not been validated at national scale.

clusters.claim: No validated, gaming-resistant SES indicator currently exists, so the quota mechanism rests on speculative, unbuilt infrastructure.
clusters.interest: Skeptical stakeholders (employers, conservatives, policy realists, independent researchers, non-state maintainers) share an interest in not committing to a nationwide reform whose central equity mechanism depends on infrastructure that does not yet exist and may not be buildable to spec.
clusters.value: This is feasibility/epistemic honesty in tension with the equity ambition the quota is meant to deliver — the claim insists you cannot bank an equity gain on an unbuilt tool.
clusters.fear: The anticipated harm is a 'theatrical' or 'paper' reform that ships an unvalidated, gameable proxy, fails to change who actually gets admitted, and either entrenches informal selection under a new label or discredits equity reform when the tool breaks.
affected: low-SES and Roma pupils (intended beneficiaries who bear the cost of a failed mechanism)
state-maintained schools and teachers (subject to compliance audits on an unbuilt system)
non-state maintainers (asked to align with a rule-set resting on missing infrastructure)
clusters.assumption: It assumes that a gaming-resistant, validated SES indicator is a genuine precondition for the quota to function — i.e., that no adequate proxy can be constructed and validated within the reform's timeline, and that resolving this precedes any decision to roll out.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is largely settled that no validated, gaming-resistant SES indicator currently exists in Hungarian administrative data (flagged as 'weak evidence'/'high cost, novel system'), but whether one can be built to acceptable false-positive/false-negative rates is genuinely unknown and only a costed multi-year pilot would resolve it.

clusters.claim: Non-state maintainers' retained admission discretion fragments any state-only reform's reach and risks a two-tier system where informal SES sorting persists.
clusters.interest: Behind this argument sits the interest of equity-oriented actors (civil society, reform advocates, parents) and employers who all want a coherent, whole-system reform whose reach is not undercut by a large opt-out sector where privileged cohorts concentrate.
clusters.value: It pits equity and system-wide fairness against institutional autonomy/subsidiarity of non-state maintainers.
clusters.fear: The driving fear is a theatrical reform that changes the label but not the machinery—informal SES sorting (interviews, reputation, family networks) simply migrates to or persists in church/foundation schools, leaving the same children excluded under a new name.
affected: families subject to the test-prep arms race
employers seeking a broad, uniformly identified talent pool
clusters.assumption: It assumes that non-state maintainers will not voluntarily align with reformed rules and that their retained discretion is substantial enough to absorb or reproduce the SES skew the reform targets.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: whether non-state opt-out actually produces a two-tier system at scale depends on maintainer behaviour and enrolment shares that have not been measured under a reformed regime, and the SES indicator needed to detect the skew does not yet exist.

clusters.claim: SES-quota reweighting overrides merit and subsidiarity by assigning admission on demographic category rather than validated ability, unfairly burdening state schools alone.
clusters.interest: State-school constituencies, meritocratic-selection defenders, and rural/non-urban stakeholders wary of urban public-school interests capturing the reform have their interests behind this argument.
clusters.value: Merit and subsidiarity (ability-based selection, decisions at the appropriate level) in tension with equity (correcting SES-skewed advantage).
clusters.fear: The fear is that admission becomes assigned by demographic category rather than validated ability, and that the burden falls asymmetrically on state schools while non-state maintainers keep discretion — producing an unfair two-tier system dressed as reform.
affected: Applicants from lower-SES backgrounds intended to benefit
clusters.assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that current merit selection validly measures ability independent of SES, so any demographic reweighting necessarily substitutes category for merit.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested and partly unknown: no validated SES indicator currently exists, the 'less coachable' assessment is flagged as weak confidence, and whether SES-skew reflects coaching advantage or unmeasured ability correlated with SES is unresolved.

clusters.claim: Non-state maintainer participation in any reformed admission rule must be genuinely voluntary with statutory autonomy protection, not enforced compliance disguised as negotiation.
clusters.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers seeking to protect their institutional admissions discretion from de facto state capture.
clusters.value: Institutional autonomy and religious/associational freedom in tension with state-driven equity standardisation.
clusters.fear: The real need is protection against being coerced into a state-designed SES-quota regime through funding or regulatory pressure while it is publicly labelled 'voluntary negotiation'.
affected: pupils and families choosing non-state schools
clusters.assumption: It assumes that a formally 'voluntary' alignment can nonetheless become effectively mandatory through funding leverage or reputational penalty unless statutory autonomy protection is explicit.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: whether alignment would in practice be enforced depends on scenario design choices not yet fixed, and the referenced SES proxy has no validated administrative indicator yet.

clusters.claim: Non-state maintainers will near-certainly expand selective intake to absorb capped demand, so S2 relocates rather than reduces early selection and the aggregate equity gain is minimal or negative.
clusters.interest: Broad coalition interest — teachers, public-school parents, equity advocates, employers, non-state maintainers, policy researchers, and the governing coalition all invoke the offset to challenge S2's core justification.
clusters.value: Equity vs. effectiveness — whether a gradual phase-down actually advances equity or merely relocates selection while claiming the equity mantle.
clusters.fear: That a decade of costly disruption yields no real reduction in early selection, leaving the public system more residual and unequal while sorting migrates to less-regulated non-state schools.
affected: public-school parents without means to navigate non-state options
the governing coalition owning the reform
clusters.assumption: That demand for selective education is not price- or supply-constrained, so capped state intake will flow to non-state maintainers who face no equivalent quota controls.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The scenario's own documentation tags the offset as 'well documented' and 'near-certain' and concedes the aggregate equity gain 'may be minimal or even negative,' so the factual core is largely settled rather than contested — the open question is only the magnitude of offset.

clusters.claim: The decade-long parallel dual-regime phase-down imposes sustained administrative and workload burden and spans electoral cycles with no precedent for durability, inviting reversal.
clusters.interest: This argument is driven by teachers, unions, and the governing coalition who would bear the sustained operational and political costs of running two admission regimes in parallel for a decade.
clusters.value: Durability and administrative proportionality are in tension with gradualist prudence: the value at stake is whether reform effort is protected against being wasted by workload burden and reversal.
clusters.fear: The real fear is that teachers invest a decade of retraining and workload adaptation, and the government spends its political capital, only to see the reform reversed by a subsequent coalition — leaving the burden borne but the goal abandoned.
affected: pupils in cohorts spanning the transition
clusters.assumption: It assumes that a 10-year dual-regime timeline is a genuine design choice rather than a fixed constraint, and that shortening it or securing a cross-party pact is feasible enough that the durability/burden objection could actually alter which scenario is chosen.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The workload/cost part is partly settled (the scenario itself tags parallel-regime administration 'medium-to-high and sustained'), but the durability part is genuinely contested-to-unknown: 'no precedent' for surviving two electoral cycles is asserted, and reversal probability cannot be quantified.

clusters.claim: S2 is managed decline that shifts burden onto general-school teachers and to the under-funded, less-regulated non-state sector without compensation, threatening employment security and public-system unity.
clusters.interest: This is driven by the interest of state-sector teachers (especially selective-track and general-track staff) and public-education-committed parents in preserving employment security, manageable workload, and a unified, non-fragmented public system.
clusters.value: The tension is public-system unity and professional/employment security (plus equity via a strong public sector) versus the political expedience of gradualism and the autonomy of the non-state sector.
clusters.fear: The real need behind the emotional framing is protection from a decade of uncompensated dual-regime workload, job insecurity and forced reassignment, and a hollowing-out of public education as selection and high-achievers migrate to a less-regulated, weaker-protected non-state sector.
affected: pupils remaining in state general schools
clusters.assumption: It assumes the non-state offset ('near-certain' per the scenario) will materialise as burden-shifting without accompanying funding, quota rules, or teacher/workload protections, and that no offsetting resource commitments will be legislated.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The core mechanical claim (non-state offset relocating rather than eliminating selection) is treated in the scenario's own record as 'well documented' and 'near-certain,' so the factual base is largely settled; what remains contested is the magnitude of teacher workload impact and the residual equity effect ('minimal or even negative').

clusters.claim: Cross-national evidence shows delayed tracking reduces SES-outcome inequality without harming mean performance, though the Hungary-specific peer-effect mechanism is weak/contested.
clusters.interest: Equity advocates, reform movements, and comprehensive-school proponents invoke the cross-national evidence to justify structural abolition of early tracking, while opponents (conservatives, employers) attack the Hungary-specific mechanism gap to resist it.
clusters.value: Equity (reducing SES-outcome inequality) versus excellence/talent-pipeline preservation, with an implicit tension between evidentiary rigor and reform ambition.
clusters.fear: Equity proponents fear that early selection permanently entrenches disadvantage for Roma and low-SES pupils; opponents fear the equity gain is speculative because the causal peer-effect mechanism is unproven in Hungary and sorting will simply relocate.
affected: employers relying on the talent pipeline
clusters.assumption: The claim assumes the cross-national delayed-tracking effect transfers to Hungary despite the acknowledged weakness of the Hungary-specific peer-effect mechanism, and that mean performance is preserved under Hungarian conditions.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The cross-national fact (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006: delayed tracking reduces SES-outcome inequality without harming mean performance) is well-evidenced, but the Hungary-specific transmission mechanism (peer effects) is explicitly tagged weak/contested by the equity expert, making it a genuine causal-mechanism uncertainty rather than settled.

clusters.claim: S3 abolishes the sorting mechanism at its root and is the only structurally coherent equity option, supportable only with binding, enforceable choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards to prevent middle-class/non-state flight relocating sorting.
clusters.interest: The interest behind this argument is that of equity-oriented reform advocates, disadvantaged/Roma pupils, families of young children, teachers, and reform-realist governance actors who want early sorting genuinely removed rather than relocated.
clusters.value: The value in tension is structural equity (removing the sorting mechanism at its root) versus middle-class choice, institutional stability, and deliverable teacher capacity.
clusters.fear: The driving fear is that abolishing comprehensive-school selection triggers middle-class and church/foundation-school flight that reconcentrates disadvantage in remaining public schools, reproducing the SES gradient in a new institutional form while teachers absorb unfunded transition risk.
affected: public comprehensive school teachers and unions
clusters.assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards can actually be legislated, enforced, and sustained across electoral cycles well enough to prevent sorting migration.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The cross-national delayed-tracking evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006) is strong, but the Hungarian peer-effect mechanism is contested/weak, teacher-retraining cost/capacity has no precise per-cohort figure (confidence low), and the magnitude of sorting migration and political durability across two cycles has no clear Hungarian precedent.

clusters.claim: S3 requires an unprecedented, costed nationwide teacher-retraining program and binding class-size caps before rollout, or it becomes a warehousing model that shifts unfunded workload onto teachers.
clusters.interest: This is driven by teachers and their unions (pedagogus_erdekvedo, pedagogus_szakszervezeti_hang) seeking that structural reform not be imposed on them without funded capacity, staffing, and autonomy protections.
clusters.value: It pits equity (abolishing early sorting) against professional dignity and feasibility (teachers' workload, autonomy, and the credibility of the reform's own equity promise if support systems are absent).
clusters.fear: The real need is protection from becoming the shock-absorber of an underfunded reform — teachers fear being held accountable for sharply increased classroom heterogeneity without paid retraining, class-size caps, or added support staff, turning comprehensiveness into a warehousing model.
affected: pupils in mixed-ability classrooms (especially disadvantaged pupils)
public comprehensive schools serving high-disadvantage populations
clusters.assumption: It assumes that without legally binding, pre-rollout costing, class-size caps, and staffing guarantees, the retraining and support will not materialize at the scale and quality S3's equity claim depends on.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: the scenario itself tags retraining cost as 'high' with 'no precise per-cohort unit cost,' the Finnish-reform expert flags Hungary as lacking equivalent teacher-quality/support systems, and no Hungarian precedent exists for retraining at this scale — so whether adequate capacity can be built in the timeline is unresolved.

clusters.claim: Abolishing early selection is a top-down imposition that removes early ability-matched specialized instruction relied on by families and the talent pipeline, with speculative equity gains and documented political-reversal risk.
clusters.interest: The interest behind this argument is that of families relying on early ability-matched instruction, the church/foundation and non-state school sector, and employers who depend on an intensive talent-identification pipeline, all seeking to preserve existing selective provision and institutional stability.
clusters.value: The tension is excellence, family choice and democratic legitimacy against a top-down equity aspiration.
clusters.fear: The driving fear is that abolition removes trusted specialized instruction and destabilizes the talent pipeline while merely relocating sorting outside democratic oversight, all for equity gains that may never materialize and a reform that may be politically reversed.
affected: employers and the talent pipeline (especially non-elite firms outside major cities)
students in remaining public comprehensive schools
clusters.assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that structural abolition alone will not be accompanied by enforceable choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards, so sorting will relocate to non-state and inter-school choice rather than be reduced.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual parts are mixed: the flight/re-sorting risk and the political-reversal precedent (Poland) are documented in the scenario, but the peer-effect mechanism underlying the equity gain is flagged 'weak/contested' and there is no Hungary-specific longitudinal evidence resolving whether non-state sorting would offset the gains.

clusters.claim: No comparator country has demonstrated that compensation offsets (rather than masks) early-selection effects at Hungary's age 10-12, so S4's core causal claim is unverified and the Portuguese precedent is non-transferable.
clusters.interest: The interest behind this argument is that of equity advocates, disadvantaged-pupil representatives, policy researchers, and reform movements who need the decision to rest on verifiable causal grounds rather than an untested transfer of the Portuguese precedent.
clusters.value: The tension is between equity (a reform that genuinely offsets early-selection harm) and administrative/political feasibility (adopting a resource-redistribution mechanism that preserves institutional continuity).
clusters.fear: The driving fear is that S4 will merely mask rather than dismantle sorting, permanently legitimizing early selection while relying on an unproven causal mechanism that leaves disadvantaged children worse off.
affected: families of children facing age-10 selection
clusters.assumption: The claim rests on the assumption that a mechanism unproven in any comparator with Hungary's early selection age (10-12) cannot be relied upon to work here, i.e., that absence of demonstrated offsetting is decision-critical rather than a gap that Hungarian evidence could fill.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: the scenario itself labels the offset-vs-mask distinction 'unverifiable ex ante' and concedes no comparator has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age, so the causal claim is genuinely unsettled rather than settled either way.

clusters.claim: S4 leaves the sorting mechanism intact and legitimizes early selection as permanent, entrenching structural inequality and removing political pressure for real reform while shifting indefinite workload onto general-school teachers.
clusters.interest: Teacher unions/labor and equity advocates (general-track teachers, Roma-rights and disadvantaged-parent groups) resisting a policy that keeps early sorting intact while loading compensatory and remedial workload onto the schools least resourced to bear it.
clusters.value: Structural equity and durable reform versus administrative continuity and cosmetic compensation—dismantling the sorting engine versus managing its downstream effects indefinitely.
clusters.fear: That compensation becomes a permanent substitute for reform: general-school teachers absorb indefinite, under-resourced high-complexity workload while sorting is legitimized as forever, and politically fragile TEIP-style funding evaporates in the first budget cut, leaving burden without resources.
affected: parents of pupils sorted out at age 10
clusters.assumption: That legitimizing compensation removes the political pressure needed for structural reform, and that compensation can at best mask rather than offset early-sorting effects—so S4 permanently entrenches selection rather than acting as a bridge to reform.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: the scenario itself states no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age (10-12) and the Portuguese TEIP precedent did not involve early tracking, so whether compensation offsets or merely masks stratification cannot be determined from available evidence; the budget-vulnerability claim rests on the scenario's own 'first line item cut' flag.

clusters.claim: TEIP-style targeted funding is historically the first line item cut during fiscal consolidation, making S4's compensatory mechanism politically fragile and unreliable without constitutional ring-fencing.
clusters.interest: The interest behind this argument is that of general-school teachers, disadvantaged and Roma pupils/families, and long-horizon planners (employers, policy researchers) who need the compensatory resources to actually persist rather than evaporate at the first budget cycle.
clusters.value: The value in tension is durability/reliability of equity provision versus fiscal-political flexibility, with an underlying equity-vs-status-quo-continuity conflict since fragile compensation leaves early sorting intact but unremedied.
clusters.fear: The real fear is that teachers will build remedial/diagnostic capacity and families will depend on compensatory resources, only to have the funding cut in a consolidation cycle — leaving permanent sorting, workload, and dependency but no resources.
affected: families of children in general schools
employers relying on the talent pipeline
future governments inheriting the entrenched system
clusters.assumption: The unstated assumption is that without constitutional or supermajority ring-fencing, ordinary legislative budget commitments to targeted funding will predictably be reversed under fiscal pressure.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core — that targeted-funding line items are historically among the first cut in fiscal consolidation — is presented as a historical pattern in the scenario and treated as a serious rather than notional risk, but whether a specific Hungarian ring-fencing mechanism would hold is unknown and contested.

clusters.claim: S4 is acceptable only if reframed as a time-limited, ring-fenced transitional bridge to structural reform (S1 or S3) with a legislated sunset clause, not as a permanent standalone substitute.
clusters.interest: This is driven by the interest of general-track teachers, disadvantaged/Roma pupils and their families, and structural-reform advocates who want compensatory spending to serve as a lever toward structural change rather than a permanent excuse to leave early selection intact.
clusters.value: Structural equity and durability (dismantling the sorting engine) in tension with administrative continuity and political feasibility (managing rather than solving early selection).
clusters.fear: The real need is protection against a decades-long trap in which selection is legitimized as permanent, teachers absorb indefinite compensating workload, and the politically fragile funding that justified the arrangement is cut in the first fiscal consolidation, leaving inequity locked in and resources gone.
affected: families of children facing age-10 selection
clusters.assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that a legislated sunset clause and ring-fenced, multi-parliamentary-term funding would actually bind future governments and force the transition to S1 or S3, rather than being repealed or ignored like ordinary budget commitments.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: the scenario itself states no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age (10-12), the Portuguese TEIP precedent involved later tracking and so is of weak transferability, and whether compensation offsets or merely masks stratification cannot be determined from available evidence.

clusters.claim: S4 is the only option that raises the floor without lowering the ceiling, preserving institutional autonomy, merit-based selective pathways, and pluralist governance while addressing SES-skew non-disruptively.
clusters.interest: Institutional stakeholders (selective gimnaziums, church maintainers, and families invested in early academic selection) who want the documented SES problem addressed without dismantling the structures and autonomy they depend on.
clusters.value: Excellence and institutional autonomy/pluralism versus equity — framed here as a claim that both can be served simultaneously ('raise the floor without lowering the ceiling').
clusters.fear: That an equity intervention becomes a Trojan horse for structural abolition, stripping institutional autonomy, admissions discretion, and merit-based pathways families rely on.
affected: families valuing early selectivity and values-alignment
clusters.assumption: That non-structural interventions (targeted funding, curriculum standards, reduced retention) can meaningfully narrow the SES gradient while selection continues to sort pupils, and that funding can be politically protected across electoral cycles.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: the Portuguese precedent supports non-structural gains, but the inputs concede no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's early selection age and that compensation 'narrows the SES gradient at best partially' and 'may mask rather than offset' sorting.
